speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 30, 1996. The survivor is Walter Austerer. The interviewer is Larry Rosenberg. We are in Rockville Center, New York State, USA. And the language is English. I am Larry Rosenberg, and today is October 30, 1996. Today we are interviewing Walter Austerer. We are in Rockville Center, New York State, USA, and the interview is in English. Could you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Walter Austerer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-L-T-E-R A-U-S-T-E-R-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names, nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 27, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 72 and 11 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-I-E-N-N-A. Austria, A-U-S-T-R-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spend some time talking about what life was like before the war. Could you tell us the name of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moses and Rose. My father was called Max, but his real name was Moses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same as mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's maiden name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Greenfield.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us a little bit about what your family life was like before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- we lived in a Jewish neighborhood, the second district in Vienna. And my grandmother lived about four blocks away from us. And there were 11 children in her family, and she had 11 children. Actually, there were two wives. But there were 11, 11 children. Two were killed in World War I, and the others were alive when I was a young child. And most of them lived fairly close to us. So there was a constant visiting. And mostly, to my grandmother, but also between uncles, and aunts, and what have you. And I had quite a few cousins. So it was a fairly lively family life, although I was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this grandmother was which, which of your parents mothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband died about a year before I was born, so I never knew him. I never knew my grandfather. And my father's family, he was born in Romania. His mother died when he was two or three years old. I don't know when his father died. He had one brother in Chernivitsi, Romania. And three brothers had emigrated to-- from Romania to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was this grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to think now. I think Hilda, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your home where you lived. Was it a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you tell us a little bit about what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small apartment. It was a one-bedroom apartment. And I slept, actually, in the-- there was a large living room. I slept in the corner. It was partitioned off where I slept. And my parents had the bedroom. And there was a kitchen and the living room.

speaker: interviewer
text: What floor was it on?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the third floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: A walk-up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: German. Tell us a little bit about some of the activities that you were involved with in your youth before the wars. Were there hobbies or sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I always like to construct things. And I always had fun doing that. And I also played a lot of soccer. And then, later on, I-- I-- one of my cousins who was a violin teacher tried to teach me the violin, and not too successfully. But I think it did instill a love of music for me. And so it was really worthwhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what schools did you go to before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a public school from six to 10, four grades. And then, I went to gymnasium, which was a-- sort of, a prep school for-- prepared you for college education. In contrast to the-- what they called the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the folk school-- where children went to the age of 14. And then after, if you wanted to enter a trade, you would then apprentice to a tailor, a shoemaker, whatever. But if you decided to get a-- have a profession, you, you went to the other one, where you had to pay. This was not a free school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the first school that you went to was, was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] . And the gymnasium, was also-- which was close by, was also called [NON-ENGLISH] gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how far were each of the schools from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about five blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Mhm. Tell me just a little bit about your friends that you had at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a few friends. My closest friend before leaving Vienna was Ziggy Diamond. And he-- we were very close at that time. I-- he left Vienna before I did. I believe he went to Palestine. I had never heard from him again. I have no idea what, what happened to him. And then, I had other friends. I had a lot of friends in school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your friends primarily Jewish? Or did you have non-Jewish friends as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Primarily Jewish, because the, the school must have been about 85%, 80% Jewish because it was a Jewish district. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your memory of the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews at, at that time when you were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- there were non-Jews in our building, and it was very comfortable one. There was no-- as far as I remember, there was no animosity. The one, the one incident that I'll always remember was, we had a gym teacher that was in gymnasium. And I, I didn't know that he was a Nazi. He, he was. And as soon as-- when-- just before the Nazis actually came in legally, he already wore a swastika button, as a lot of others did. And-- nut he took us on an outing, a day's outing, and he got lost. We went into the Alps, into the forerunners of the Alps, and he got lost. And then, of course, for him that was a very unpleasant experience. After all, he considered himself a very proper person and a knowledgeable person, and so on and so forth. So-- and we had to do a lot of hiking. We had-- in order to get back, we had probably hiked twice as much as we should have. And it was pretty strenuous. And so at the end of the day, he remarked, well, even in this school, I can see that there are-- that there are people who can take that kind of strenuous exercise, meaning that the Jews can also-- [LAUGHS] --manage to hike those distances. And I always remember that because it was really the first time that a teacher came out with that kind of a remark. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old where you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- what sort of impression did that make on you when you heard that remark, the intonation of the anti-Semitism? How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't-- as I said, we were isolated in that school, really. I'm, I'm sure pupils have been in-- children who had been in different districts, which were, you know, maybe, mostly Gentile or had a different mixture of Jews and Gentile would have heard that many times before. But for us, as I said, you know, we were really isolated in that respect. And on the other hand, anti-Semitism was all around us. It was in the newspapers. It was written about. It was talked about. So it was not a unique experience. But in this school, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about the generalized anti-Semitism that you observed in Vienna at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, I, I don't really-- again, because I lived in the Jewish district. I, myself, didn't experience it until Hitler came in, until they actually marched in. But also because-- probably because I was blond. I had blond hair. I had blue eyes. I didn't look Jewish. So I could get away with an awful lot. And I'm sure that, that also prevented me from, from encountering it before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war and before--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think we should say before Hitler marched in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Before--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Hitler marched in in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, were there any restrictions on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not before, no. No, not that I experienced. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit more about your home life. What was the, the means of support of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was in the textile business til about 1932, maybe. And then, the depression, which actually-- worldwide depression-- which actually started in Austria because of the Creditanstalt Bank, which went bust, and then created the domino theory. So things were terrible. The unemployment rate was 20% or 25%. And he-- his firm went out of business. He lost his job. And things went very, very bad, economically, for us for a couple of years. Very bad. He tried to, to sell on his own. It didn't work out. And finally then, maybe in 1933 or 1934, he went into the restaurant supply business and began to build up that business again. And it was beginning to be fairly successful when he finally had to stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not what-- for awhile, we observed the Sabbath. And we did attend the services, the high holy days, and also, occasionally, during the year. But not consistently, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- do you remember what synagogue or shul you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- I went-- there was a temple called the Polish Temple across the street from our building, which was burned down during Kristallnacht. And now there's a plaque in-- on the building that they elected there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the temple was again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish Temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called the Polish Temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what street was it on? Do you remember the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on Leopoldstrasse, right across the street from us. And as a matter of fact, we could watch the, the choir rehearse and-- because it was that close. We could look into that window where they rehearsed. And-- but I was not bar mitvahed there That was a unorthodox one. And I was bar mitvahed within a conservative one. And I don't recall the name now. A Very large synagogue in the-- near, near Praterstrasse. But I don't remember the name of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you have any formal religious training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in school, religion was a subject that was taught in school. So two or three times a week-- I forgot now, maybe twice a week-- we attended a class in religion. And then I also attended, for about a couple of years, I attended, in addition, which was optional, in the afternoon, a Hebrew class, modern Hebrew class. So I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what was some of the subjects you studied, both, in the public school, as well as in the afternoon school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean general?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. The, the religious topics.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the religious topics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Bible, mostly. That was taught in the, in the religious school, in the religious class. And as I said, the optional taught modern Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the training for your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- that, again, was done by that Hebrew teacher who taught us in school, and-- the religious teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had to perform. And then, afterwards, we had a party in my apartment. And in those days, you got books and fountain pens, mostly. [LAUGHS] There were about 12 kids. My mother did the cooking. And uncles and aunts came. And that was the, the party. And I think it was more meaningful then what is being done today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was your-- was your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: In November?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what you actually did in, in the service, what, what role you took?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly anymore now. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during the holidays, the Jewish holidays, what-- can you remember the celebrations that you had? How-- how you participated in the holidays through the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went with my father always. As a matter of fact, on the high holy days, he went to another shul, which was close to my grandmother's house on [NON-ENGLISH], which isn't there anymore. They've also been destroyed. And I remember once, I was quite young. And I went out into-- there was also a courtyard with chestnut trees. And during the service, another friend of mine and myself-- well, maybe he wasn't a friend. Maybe it was just kids, other kids there. And we trying to knock down the chestnuts in the, the, that yard from trees. And my father was looking for me. And he came out. And he was furious, of course. And I felt terribly guilty. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember that. And how about Yom Kippur? Did you-- do you have any memories of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was on Yom Kippur. Yeah. It took place in Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we always went to my grandmother for-- afterwards, for the breakfast.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it just your family or all your cousins came and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few of the cousins, yeah. Not all, but quite a few of the children and grandchildren were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you break the fast? Would it be a lot of food? Or what--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was much less. Just, just-- I forgot what she prepared, actually. I do remember that, the children-- she had two huge cans in her living room. Like, milk cans. Maybe they were milk cans. And one was filled with plum jam, and the other one was filled with goose fat. And when the children came, this is what you got. You Had your choice. So either taking slice of bread and, and putting that goose fat on it or the plum jam.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about some of the other holidays? How, how was Passover celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We usually made Passover. Yeah. We had it in our apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any memories of the service?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember a couple of years, my father invited destitute people. I mean, we'd almost call them homeless people. And they actually invited them to the seder. I still remember that. And I was very impressed by that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Any, any other holidays or celebrations in the family that come to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were birthdays celebrated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birthday was always celebrated. And I was always looking forward to it. And I was always hoping to get another addition to my erector set. And sometimes I did, and sometimes I didn't. When things were terrible, I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I always got a present. Yes, Hanukkah I remember, too. I remember, actually-- with my mother, my father traveled a lot when he was in the textile business. But in the making-- what would we have made? It was lead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we formed-- maybe we formed dreidels, and I think we did. I forgot now how we did it, but I do remember doing it. Yeah. And celebrating Hanukkah. That I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many songs that were sung?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit, yeah. Yeah, a couple of the, you know, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you sang them in Hebrew or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And other festival memories that, that come back to mind from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I do remember, they did have-- we had-- the apartment house had a huge-- there were four courtyards, four apartment, apartment buildings who all had their courtyards. And then, formed a square of courtyards. And they would have been-- in one of them, they always had a succah. But we didn't go there. There  was another building. And we didn't go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who built the succah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess the people who lived in that particular building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that very common? Or, or was that, you know, just something that happened in rare places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the-- in that Jewish district, it would've been fairly common, where there was an opportunity, where there was a courtyard. Not every building had that kind of a courtyard. And-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you, you, you would look forward to getting your erector set parts. Was that a big thing for you? Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a very big thing for me. And as a matter of fact, I-- because I loved to build. And as a matter of fact, when my young son was about eight years-- oh, maybe, eight-- I don't remember. Seven or eight. And I found out that Vienna still had those sets. They were still making them. And I wrote to a cousin of mine who lives there now and went back after the war. And I told her to buy it for me. And of course, I bought the largest one that existed. You know, my, my dream. And so I remember I was very excited and hoping that my younger son would be as interested in it as I was. And he used it a little bit. But then I, eventually, when he was sick, I built him an enormous carousel. I think that was about the last either one of us did with it, but my wish had been fulfilled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living in Vienna, at that time, what was-- what was some of the-- were there any cultural aspects of the city that you were able to take advantage of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not very much because-- I did go to the theater a few times, and that I loved. Actually, I don't remember going to a concert. And chances are, I wouldn't have it at that age. But also because, as I said before, at age 10, 11, my father-- the depression was at its worst, and we really couldn't afford to, to buy tickets. That was the last thing in a world that, that-- enough difficulty to just put food on the table. And then, of course, year later, Hitler-- a couple years later, Hitler came in. So there wasn't much of a chance. But I did go to the theater. And I still remember my first play, which was which I enjoyed enormously. And then, I saw a couple of others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of books in your homes? In your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few. Books, again, when you think about that, books were not readily available. And I loved books. And there was a, a private library. I don't remember where there were any public ones. I don't believe there were, except maybe the university library. But there were little private libraries. And I used those all the time. And there was one-- there were two books that would come out. Like, an encyclopedia came out every year. One was scientific, and the other one was just travel and general. And I was always looking forward to the next issue. I couldn't wait to, to get those. And sometimes I would-- a couple of times, I think, my father bought them for me. Then, I remember when he was-- he had promised me that he would buy it for me, and, and-- the next one-- because I had done extremely well in school. I was on top of tying for second place, I think. And then, he lost his job. And, and, you know, things were terrible. And I never got that book, and I was terribly disappointed. But I loved books and always read a lot of books, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read any fiction? Any of the classics?

speaker: interviewee
text: We read all of that. And we had-- I had an excellent education in gymnasium. It was a marvelous foundation. I had almost five years of Latin and all the German writers. [NON-ENGLISH] And we read all of those, and the Greek mythology. Yeah. It was an amazing education for, for-- when you think about it, for a 11-year-old, 10-year-old, 11. I was even younger because I still got into that class. My birthday was late November, but I-- so I was younger than a lot of the kids in my class and almost a year ahead. And to have that education, which, which was wonderful and I-- was such a big help when I came to this country because it was a foundation that had been laid, which allowed you to, to learn a lot afterwards, and no matter in what language or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you study any languages other than German language?

speaker: interviewee
text: French, a little bit of French. We were beginning to learn French. And then, of course, Hebrew, which I, you know, knew fairly well. You mentioned earlier that one of your teachers was a Nazi, was wearing a swastika. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Nazis came into Austria, were there any other, other Nazis that you knew? Were there any other people that were becoming apparent to you that were Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. For Instance, the doctor. There was a doctor in my grandmother's house who we knew was a Nazi, even when it was illegal to be a Nazi. He, after the war, committed suicide, from what I  had heard. And there were other people, yes, whom you knew who were Nazis. Austria was social democracy at that time. Until about '37, maybe. '36 or '37. And then, fascist-- sort of a semo-fascist government came in. But yes, there were a lot of Nazis around.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop here for a moment.
